metadata.subject: Digital illustration of a photorealistic blue eye, in oil paining style
metadata.expanded_subject: An oil painting-esque digital illustration of a dark blue eye, focusing on soft washes of blue and subtle shading to capture the depth and texture of the iris.

painting_plan.subject: Digital illustration of a photorealistic blue eye, in oil painting style
painting_plan.expanded_subject: An oil painting-esque digital illustration of a dark blue eye, focusing on soft washes of blue and subtle shading to capture the depth and texture of the iris.

layers.name: Dark ground background
layers.description: Establish a deep, warm-dark toned background covering the entire 800x600 canvas. This acts as the traditional oil painting ground, using deep charcoal browns and near-blacks to set the moody, intimate atmosphere. The tone should be slightly warmer at the center where the eye will sit, fading to cooler dark edges.
layers.techniques: Broad sweeping strokes across the entire canvas, blending wet-brush passes to create a smooth but slightly textured ground. Apply dry-brush at edges for a vignette-like darkening.
layers.shapes: Horizontal and diagonal broad bands filling the canvas, subtle radial gradient effect centered around canvas midpoint (400,300).
layers.highlights: Keep the very center of the canvas (around 400,300) marginally lighter at approximately #1C1A14 to suggest a warm glow beneath the eye. Edges should be deepest black.

layers.name: Skin and orbital base
layers.description: Paint the surrounding skin, eyelids, and orbital area of the eye. The eye should be centered at approximately (400, 300) with the almond shape spanning roughly 500px wide and 200px tall. Lay in the base skin tones of the upper and lower eyelids, brow area, and surrounding flesh, establishing the overall form and shadow masses of the eye socket.
layers.techniques: Broad wet-brush strokes to block in large skin tone masses. Use dry-brush to blend transitions between light and shadow on the eyelids. Apply darker tones in the eye socket crease and lower lid shadow areas first.
layers.shapes: Curved almond-shaped eyelid forms, broad organic masses for brow and cheek areas, soft curved shadow bands in the eye socket crease.
layers.highlights: Upper eyelid should be darker (shadow from brow), lower lid catches more light. The inner and outer corners of the eye should be deepest in shadow. Keep brushwork loose and painterly.

layers.name: Sclera and eye white base
layers.description: Paint the white of the eye (sclera) visible on either side of the iris, within the almond eye opening. The sclera is not pure white but a warm off-white with subtle pink and grey tones, especially near the inner corner (tear duct area) and along the lower lid. Cover the visible sclera region within the eye opening.
layers.techniques: Smooth wet-brush application for the main sclera body, blending to avoid harsh edges. Use dry-brush to add subtle texture and warmth variations. Slightly darker near the iris edge and corners.
layers.shapes: Curved triangular wedge shapes on left and right sides of the iris, following the curvature of the eye opening.
layers.highlights: The sclera is slightly brighter toward the center-lower area where light hits. Add a very subtle pink-grey tone near the inner corner for the tear duct. Avoid pure white to maintain realism.

layers.name: Iris base and pupil
layers.description: Paint the iris as a circle centered at approximately (400, 295), roughly 160px in diameter. Lay in the deep dark blue base of the iris with rich, dark navy tones. Paint the pupil as a deep black circle at the center of the iris, approximately 60px in diameter. Establish the fundamental color mass of the iris.
layers.techniques: Use circular wet-brush strokes following the radial structure of the iris. Apply darker tones toward the outer limbal ring and around the pupil. Build up rich dark navy layers with slightly varied hue to suggest depth.
layers.shapes: Concentric circular forms, radial spoke-like strokes emanating from the pupil outward, solid dark circle for the pupil.
layers.highlights: The outer edge of the iris (limbal ring) should be darkest, nearly black. The area just outside the pupil should be slightly lighter navy to suggest the iris catching light. Keep the pupil a deep matte black.

layers.name: Iris texture and depth
layers.description: Add the complex fibrous texture of the iris over the base layer. Paint fine radial filaments, crypts, and collarette details in varying shades of blue, teal, and steel. This layer builds the characteristic spoke-and-web texture of a blue iris, adding mid-tone blues and hints of grey-green to create visual complexity and depth.
layers.techniques: Fine radiating line strokes from pupil edge outward for fibrous texture. Use chalk strokes for soft, diffuse mid-tone areas. Arc strokes for the collarette ring. Dry-brush for subtle blended texture masses.
layers.shapes: Fine radial lines emanating from pupil, concentric arc for the collarette (inner iris ring at about 80px from center), small irregular oval crypts scattered in the iris.
layers.highlights: Concentrate brighter blue-teal filaments in the upper-left quadrant of the iris where light would hit. The collarette ring should be slightly lighter and more defined. Keep texture subtle and layered, not overly uniform.

layers.name: Eyelashes, eyelid details and skin refinement
layers.description: Paint the upper and lower eyelashes as fine curved lines arcing outward from the eyelid margins. Refine the eyelid skin with additional tonal layers, adding the subtle warm highlight on the upper eyelid fold and the shadow cast by the upper lashes onto the iris. Add the subtle pink-red of the inner corner and tear duct. Refine skin texture with light dry-brush passes.
layers.techniques: Fine curved line strokes for individual lashes, varying length and curve. Use burn technique to deepen the lash line and shadow beneath upper lashes. Dry-brush for skin texture refinement and soft eyelid highlights.
layers.shapes: Long curved arc lines for upper lashes fanning outward and upward, shorter curved lines for lower lashes. Soft curved highlight band on upper eyelid fold.
layers.highlights: Upper lashes should be densest and darkest at the lash line, fanning outward. Cast shadow from upper lashes falls on the upper iris. Add a warm highlight on the upper eyelid fold to suggest the rounded form of the eye.

layers.name: Specular highlights and final luminosity
layers.description: Add the final specular highlights and luminous accents that bring the eye to life. Paint the primary catchlight as a bright white-blue irregular shape in the upper-left area of the iris and pupil (approximately at position 370, 265). Add secondary softer reflections. Add subtle blue-white limbal ring glow. Final wet-brush glazes of deep blue over the iris to unify and deepen the color.
layers.techniques: Use dodge technique for the brightest specular catchlight to create a luminous glow effect. Wet-brush for soft secondary reflections and the limbal ring glow. Small splatter for micro-highlights suggesting moisture on the eye surface.
layers.shapes: Irregular bright polygon shape for primary catchlight, small soft circle for secondary catchlight on opposite side, thin arc of light along the lower iris edge (reflection of lower eyelid), subtle circular glow around the limbal ring.
layers.highlights: The primary catchlight is the most important element — it should be bright white-blue and positioned upper-left of the iris. A secondary smaller, softer catchlight can appear lower-right. The wet surface of the eye should have a subtle overall sheen. These final touches transform the eye from flat to three-dimensional and alive.

painting_plan.overall_notes: This layer plan follows classical oil painting principles adapted for digital illustration. The dark ground approach (layer 1) is characteristic of Old Masters technique, allowing subsequent layers to glow against the dark base. The sequence moves from large dark masses to progressively finer details, mimicking how oil painters work fat-over-lean. The blue iris palette centers on deep navy and steel blues (#0A1A3A to #3A7AAA range) with warm skin tones providing complementary contrast. The final specular highlights in layer 7 are critical for photorealism — the catchlight is what makes an eye appear alive. Throughout all layers, maintain loose, confident brushwork visible in the stroke texture to preserve the oil painting aesthetic rather than achieving a smooth digital look.

strokes.batch_reasoning: This final batch for Layer 1 completes the dark ground. Two horizontal dry-brush bands sweep across the upper and lower thirds of the canvas in near-black tones to fill remaining gaps. Two diagonal dry-brush strokes reinforce the left and right edge vignette darkening, pushing the corners to deepest black as required. The filled circle at the canvas center (400,300) with the warmer #1C1A14 tone establishes the subtle warm glow beneath where the eye will sit — a classical oil painting ground technique. Together these strokes complete all objectives: full canvas coverage, textured dry-brush quality, cooler darker edges, and a marginally warmer center.

This batch establishes the core skin and orbital masses for Layer 2. Stroke 1 is a broad dark dry-brush sweep across the upper region (brow/upper eyelid shadow zone) in warm dark brown, establishing the shadow mass above the eye. Stroke 2 is a lighter dry-brush band along the lower lid area in mid-skin tone, suggesting the lighter lower eyelid catching light. Stroke 3 is a thick arc tracing the eye socket crease in deep shadow brown, defining the almond orbital shape from below. Stroke 4 is a dry-brush pass along the lash line area in deep shadow tone to deepen the eye socket crease. Stroke 5 is a chalk stroke along the lower lid in warm light skin tone to suggest the illuminated lower eyelid, keeping the brushwork loose and painterly as required.

This batch focuses on completing the orbital and eyelid skin structure. Stroke 1 is a dark arc along the upper eye socket crease to deepen the shadow mass under the brow — the darkest tone anchors the upper lid. Stroke 2 is a dry-brush sweep along the upper eyelid edge in deep brown to reinforce the lash-line shadow and eyelid fold. Stroke 3 is a lighter dry-brush along the lower lid area to establish the lighter skin tone that catches illumination below the eye. Stroke 4 is a mid-tone arc along the lower orbital rim to suggest the rounded form of the eye socket and cheek transition. Stroke 5 is a chalk pass across the upper eyelid in warm flesh tone to add painterly skin texture and begin suggesting the upper eyelid highlight fold, consistent with oil painting technique.

Stroke 3 is a lighter dry-brush along the lower lid area to establish the lighter skin tone that catches illumination below the eye. This final pass on Layer 2 adds: (1) a warm flesh-tone splatter across the upper orbital area to create painterly skin texture variation; (2) a dark arc defining the upper eyelid crease shadow, deepening the eye socket form; (3) a dry-brush sweep along the lower lid to establish the lighter, light-catching lower eyelid tone; (4) a chalk stroke along the upper eyelid margin in medium brown to reinforce the eyelid fold shadow and brow shadow mass; (5) a dark arc along the lower orbital shadow to deepen the inner corner and lower socket shadow. Together these complete the skin and orbital base with proper light/shadow distribution — darker upper lid, lighter lower lid, deep corners — finishing Layer 2.

This batch lays in the sclera (eye white) on both sides of where the iris will sit. Two broad dry-brush strokes cover the left and right sclera wedge regions with warm off-white (#D8D0C8), following the almond eye opening shape. Two arc strokes reinforce the curved inner edge of the sclera near where the iris boundary will be, using a slightly warmer/greyer tone (#C8BEB4) to suggest the sclera darkening near the iris limbal edge. A pink-grey splatter near the inner corner (around x=185) adds the subtle warm-pink tone of the tear duct area, maintaining realism and avoiding pure white. All strokes use the recommended sclera palette and wet-brush/dry-brush techniques.

This batch establishes the fundamental iris structure for Layer 4. Stroke 1 lays in the deep dark navy base fill for the full iris circle (80px radius) at center (400,295). Stroke 2 adds a thick dark outline at the limbal ring edge to create the characteristic darkest-outer-edge of the iris. Stroke 3 adds a slightly lighter navy mid-tone in the inner iris zone (60px radius) to suggest the iris catching light just outside the pupil. Stroke 4 is a sweeping arc in the lower portion of the iris to add tonal depth and suggest the shadowed lower iris. Stroke 5 paints the pupil as a solid near-black filled circle (30px radius) at the iris center, establishing the deep matte black pupil. Together these strokes build the essential color mass and structural foundation of the iris as specified in the layer plan.

This batch builds the iris fibrous texture characteristic of a blue eye in oil painting style. Two radial line strokes extend from the pupil outward — one upper-left (catching light) and one upper-right — simulating the spoke-like filaments of the iris stroma. A concentric arc stroke traces the collarette ring (the inner iris boundary) in a brighter blue-teal to define that anatomical structure. A chalk stroke along the collarette area adds soft, diffuse mid-tone blue texture suggesting the fibrous mass of the iris. A dry-brush stroke across the mid-iris adds a subtle blended texture band. Together these strokes build the layered, complex look of a real iris while maintaining the oil painting aesthetic.

This batch focuses on Layer 6 objectives: (1) A broad dark arc reinforces the upper eyelid lash line margin, the densest darkest part of the lash base. (2-3) Two flanking arc segments add lash-line density at the medial and lateral upper lid corners where lashes cluster. (4) A dry-brush pass in warm skin tone (#C49070) along the upper eyelid fold adds the soft warm highlight suggesting the rounded eyelid form, characteristic of oil painting skin rendering. (5) A lower lid lash arc in near-black traces the lower eyelid margin, beginning the lower lash definition. Together these strokes build the eyelid structure and begin the lash layer in an oil-painting style.
